# Bayes for a continuous parameter

Last module the parameter took one of three values. Now it is **continuous**: the
mean jaw length $\mu$ of a fish species could be *any* number. The logic is
identical, but the sum in the denominator becomes an **integral**.

## The same theorem, continuous version

$$
p(\mu \mid y) = \frac{p(y \mid \mu)\,p(\mu)}{\displaystyle\int p(y \mid \mu)\,p(\mu)\,d\mu}
$$

We measure a handful of jaws with a known measurement error (a fixed standard
deviation $\sigma$). The **likelihood** $p(y\mid\mu)$ peaks near the sample mean;
the **prior** $p(\mu)$ encodes what we expected beforehand.

## Why the integral?

The denominator sums `prior × likelihood` over *every possible* $\mu$. With a
continuous parameter there are infinitely many values, so we cannot add them up
one by one. Instead we **chop the $\mu$-axis into thin slices**, add up
`prior × likelihood × (slice width)`, and let the slices get thinner and
thinner. That limit *is* the integral.

The good news: to find the **most probable** value — the **maximum a posteriori
(MAP)** estimate — we do not even need the denominator. It is a constant that
does not depend on $\mu$, so the peak of `prior × likelihood` is the peak of the
posterior.

## Your task

Drag the slider to the peak of the posterior (purple). Watch how the prior (cyan)
and likelihood (orange) combine. Then switch to a tighter prior and find the new
MAP — the prior pulls the answer toward itself.
